name: Cipher Digital Inc.

profile.description: Cipher Digital (formerly Cipher Mining, rebranded February 2026) is an industrial-scale data center construction and operations company allocating capacity between Bitcoin mining and high-performance computing (AI/HPC). The company has secured approximately $11.4B in contracted data center revenue through long-term leases with hyperscalers including AWS and Google, with first HPC revenue expected in H2 2026.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Cipher successfully pivoted from a pure Bitcoin mining operation to HPC data center infrastructure, securing $11.4B in contracted revenue with tier-1 hyperscalers including AWS (Black Pearl, 300 MW, 15-year lease) and Google/Fluidstack (Barber Lake, 300 MW, 10-year lease), representing a transformative business model reinvention [1]. AI Deployment (1/2): The Barber Lake and Black Pearl facilities are purpose-built for AI/HPC workloads, directly enabling hyperscaler AI infrastructure, though HPC revenue has not yet commenced (expected H2 2026) [1]. Market Share (1/2): Among the early Bitcoin miners to establish credible HPC data center operations, but competes with Core Scientific, Applied Digital, and established data center operators for hyperscaler contracts [2]. Pipeline (1/2): Additional development sites beyond the initial 600 MW contracted capacity are under evaluation, and CEO Page cited pricing power in lease negotiations [2]. R&D Intensity (1/1): The full company rebrand from Cipher Mining to Cipher Digital and the hiring of Lee Bratcher and Drew Armstrong for AI/HPC expansion reflect committed strategic investment.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Cipher's mission of 'developing and operating industrial-scale data centers engineered for next-generation compute' is clear and purposeful but functionally generic — any data center company could claim the same [2]. Community Impact (1/2): Data centers create local jobs and tax revenue in Texas and other locations, and the company emphasizes use of renewable energy sources (wind and solar in Texas), but no specific charitable programs or community investment dollar amounts are disclosed [3]. Employee Retention (0/2): Only 66 employees managing multi-billion-dollar construction projects; CFO transition from Ed Farrell to Greg Mumford in October 2025 was orderly but no retention data available [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Tyler Page holds significant equity compensation (~95% stock-based), aligning interests with shareholders, but recent insider transactions show net selling — Page sold $604K, director Grossman sold $436K, and director Newsome sold $711K in March 2026.

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Cipher's current financials are distressed as it bridges from mining revenue to HPC lease income. Q1 2026 revenue was $34.8M (down from $60M in Q4 2025) as mining operations wind down at Black Pearl, with net loss of $114M [1]. D/E of 641.6% reflects $4.75B in debt (high-yield bonds funding data center construction) against negative equity. ROE is deeply negative at -121.7%. Profit margin is approximately 0% on a TTM basis, masking large quarterly swings. Forward P/E of 45x assumes $0.46 forward EPS from HPC lease commencement in H2 2026 [1]. The massive contracted backlog ($11.4B) provides future visibility but zero current HPC revenue. Liquidity of $715M cash provides construction runway. Liquidity floor cleared: 26.6M avg volume x $20.55 = $546.7M daily [1].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Cipher's pivot to HPC data center infrastructure makes AI an existential enabler rather than a threat — the entire business model is built on providing physical compute infrastructure for AI workloads [1][2]. AI data centers require massive power, physical construction, and proximity to energy sources — capabilities that AI itself cannot replicate. The 10-15 year take-or-pay lease structures with hyperscalers provide revenue certainty regardless of AI model evolution. The company is immune to AI disruption because it IS AI infrastructure. AI Opportunity (1/1): Direct AI revenue exposure through $11.4B contracted HPC leases with Google/Fluidstack and AWS, with Barber Lake delivery commencing in H2 2026 and analyst consensus projecting $835M revenue by FY2027 [1].

infinite_game.summary: Cipher demonstrates meaningful infinite game qualities through its transformative business model pivot and long-term lease structures (10-15 years), but the company is too young and the transformation too recent to validate durability. The pivot appears driven more by economic opportunity than transcendent purpose, and the 66-person organization must prove it can execute multi-billion-dollar data center builds over the next decade.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Cipher's vision of building critical AI infrastructure for the next computing era connects to a genuine purpose beyond mining Bitcoin. CEO Page's emphasis on 'powering the digital economy through critical infrastructure' and the complete company rebrand demonstrate commitment to a larger mission [2]. However, the pivot was clearly driven by superior HPC economics rather than a pre-existing vision, and the cause has not been tested through adversity or multi-decade consistency.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): The leadership team includes experienced operators from technology, fintech, and energy domains — co-Presidents Kelly and Iwaschuk, CFO Mumford, and recent hires Bratcher and Armstrong for policy and strategic projects [2]. The CFO transition was orderly. However, with only 66 employees managing multi-billion-dollar construction projects, the organization is stretched thin and heavily dependent on key individuals.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Cipher competes with Core Scientific, Applied Digital, and traditional data center operators like Equinix for hyperscaler contracts. CEO Page's commentary about 'pricing power in data center lease negotiations' and the company's willingness to partner with (rather than compete against) hyperscalers like AWS and Google demonstrates a healthy competitive awareness [2].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): The complete strategic transformation from Bitcoin mining to HPC data centers — including rebranding from Cipher Mining to Cipher Digital, winding down mining operations at Black Pearl, and committing to $4.75B in construction debt — represents textbook existential flexibility [1][2]. The company recognized the superior economics and secular demand for AI infrastructure and pivoted decisively before many peers, even at the cost of near-term revenue disruption (Q1 2026 revenue down 29% as mining winds down).

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Raising $4.75B in high-yield bonds to fund multi-hundred-megawatt data center construction while winding down the existing mining business requires significant conviction and willingness to accept near-term pain (Q1 2026 net loss of $114M) for long-term opportunity [1]. However, the decision was validated by overwhelming market demand for AI infrastructure, reducing the perceived courage — this was a well-supported bet rather than a contrarian stance.

investment_thesis: Cipher Digital has secured $11.4B in contracted HPC data center revenue through long-term take-or-pay leases with AWS and Google, with first material HPC revenue commencing in H2 2026 at the Barber Lake facility [1]. Analyst consensus projects revenue surging to $835M by FY2027 (from $210M in 2025) as contracted capacity comes online, with EPS reaching $0.36 by Q1 2027 [1]. The company holds $715M in cash liquidity, CEO Page touts pricing power in lease negotiations, and the stock's $8.4B market cap reflects growing confidence in the data center pivot. At 45x forward P/E on nascent HPC earnings, significant upside exists if execution delivers on the contracted backlog [1][2].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on extreme leverage and execution risk: $4.75B in debt (D/E of 641.6%) against $715M cash means any construction delay at Barber Lake or Black Pearl could strain liquidity and trigger debt service concerns [1]. Q1 2026 revenue of $34.8M (down 29% as mining winds down) with -$114M net loss demonstrates the painful 'dead zone' between mining decline and HPC ramp. ROE is deeply negative at -121.7%. Insider selling is notable — CEO Page sold $604K, director Grossman $436K, and director Newsome $711K in March 2026 alone. Customer concentration risk is extreme: two hyperscaler relationships (AWS and Google) drive the entire $11.4B contracted backlog. Free cash flow is massively negative at -$2.95B as construction spending accelerates [1].
key_catalysts: Barber Lake 300 MW facility reaching substantial completion in H2 2026, commencing first material HPC revenue from the Google/Fluidstack lease [1]
AWS Black Pearl 300 MW delivery with rent commencing — transforming contracted backlog into recognized revenue and cash flow [1]
Analyst revenue projection of $835M for FY2027 (4x current revenue) as contracted capacity comes online, potentially driving re-rating toward data center peer multiples [1]
key_risks: D/E of 641.6% with $4.75B in debt creates existential leverage risk — construction delays or cost overruns could exhaust the $715M cash position before HPC revenue commences [1]
Customer concentration risk: AWS and Google drive the entire $11.4B contracted backlog, and loss of either relationship would be catastrophic to equity value
Notable insider selling by CEO Page ($604K), directors Grossman ($436K) and Newsome ($711K) in March 2026 contradicts the bullish transformation narrative
sources: Cipher Digital Q1 2026 Earnings Release and 10-Q — $34.8M revenue, -$114M net loss, $11.4B contracted revenue, $715M cash, May 5, 2026
Cipher Digital leadership, rebrand, AI/HPC strategy — CEO Tyler Page commentary, Sherwood News, StockTitan, 2025-2026
